title: Community Health Worker (CHW) Sustainability and allied professions in Arizona

summary.hook: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously worked as Behavioral Health Technicians (BHTs), peer recovery support specialists (PRSS), or other behavioral health paraprofessionals.
summary.summary_30s: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously worked as Behavioral Health Technicians (BHTs), peer recovery support specialists (PRSS), or other behavioral health paraprofessionals. The study examines how these trainees perceive CHW roles relative to their prior roles, including overlap, boundaries, training requirements, and workforce experiences when cross-trained. With 9 participants interviewed so far, respondents describe major variability in how CHWs are positioned within healthcare organizations, frequent role ambiguity and limited recognition, and workforce sustainability concerns (grant-funded instability, inadequate pay), suggesting a need for clearer role delineation, standardized training pathways, and expanded reimbursement mechanisms.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Define roles of CHWs, BHTs, and PRSS to identify overlapping responsibilities and professional boundaries.

Approach: Population: Trainees enrolled in the ASU CHW Training Program who reported prior experience as BHTs, PRSS, or other behavioral health paraprofessionals. Primary outcome: Themes describing role perceptions, overlap/boundaries, training and workforce experiences, and sustainability challenges.
Finding: Data collection ongoing; 9 participants interviewed to date.

Why it matters: * Authors’ stated conclusions: Participants described substantial variability in how CHWs are positioned within healthcare organizations, including differences in authority, recognition, and collaboration with behavioral health and peer support professionals.
key_numbers: * n=9 interviews completed to date (data collection ongoing).
Nine participants interviewed to date; ages 25–62; 7 females.
Participants identified as White (2), Hispanic (4), Black (2), Other (1).
Frequent themes include organizational role hierarchy (7 mentions) and workforce sustainability/compensation (6 mentions).
Compensation perceived as inadequate; suggested wage range $20–$25/hour.
With 9 participants interviewed so far, respondents describe major variability in how CHWs are positioned within healthcare organizations, frequent role ambiguity and limited re...
takeaways: Data collection ongoing; 9 participants interviewed to date.

Poster includes a “theme” table summarizing categories and participant count per theme (exact counts are readable for each row) and a note: “4 out of 9 interviewees mentioned doing this to help people and as a new role.”

Authors’ stated conclusions: Participants described substantial variability in how CHWs are positioned within healthcare organizations, including differences in authority, recognition, and collaboration with behavioral health and peer support professionals. Role ambiguity and unclear role definitions may limit effective integration into multidisciplinary teams. Findings suggest the need for clearer role delineation, standardized training pathways, and stronger institutional recognition, with workforce sustainability supported by expanded reimbursement mechanisms, stronger career advancement pathways, and fair/consistent compensation.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: How many total interviews are planned, and what is the stopping rule (saturation criteria)?

what_to_ask.question: “Organizational structure and role hierarchy” had 7 mentions—what were the most common examples of limited authority or recognition for CHWs?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How are you ensuring findings aren’t driven by the unique culture of one training program (triangulation with other CHW groups or employers)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What specific role delineation tools would help (job descriptions, competency frameworks, supervision models)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Expand the sample to include employers/clinical supervisors to compare perspectives on role boundaries

collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Compare across regions of Arizona (metro Phoenix vs rural/tribal communities)
